text: Sterile drapes expose the patient's trunk from the sternal manubrium to the pubic symphysis. The surgeon performs an exifopubic median laparotomy to access the abdominal cavity, preparing to control any lesions. Visible equipment includes air pulsated heating blankets, a scalpel, retractors, aortic clamps for the splenic hilum, blood products, a cell saver, surgical suctions, and swabs.

The surgeon begins by eviscerating the small bowel to rapidly expose the abdominal cavity. A reniform bowl is used to scoop out the hemoperitoneum, which is set aside for treatment and reinfusion. The surgeon then performs general primary packing of the abdominal cavity, starting with the most critical bleeder and continuing in a clockwise manner through the left hypochondrium, right hypochondrium, left lumbar, left iliac fossa, pelvis, right iliac fossa, right lumbar, and umbilical regions.

The surgeon exposes the spleen and carefully clamps the splenic hilum to avoid damaging the caudopancreatus. The splenocolic ligament is divided, providing access to the posterior side of the spleen. The short gastric vessels are clamped, ligated, and divided in stages, allowing for the ligation and sectioning of the splenic hilum.

The surgeon examines the complete abdominal cavity, including the omental bursa, systematically searching for any lesions. The small bowel, colon, retroperitoneum, and diaphragm are inspected. A Radon's drain is placed in the left hypochondrium near the caudal pancreas to manage potential iatrogenic fistulas. The cavity is cleansed thoroughly with physiological serum at 40 degrees Celsius.
